risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Those who agreed to participate were randomized to either the Standard Treatment group (STD; n=49) or to the Standard Treatment + Cognitive-Behavioral Treatment group (STD+CBT; n=52) using a computerized urn randomization procedure [28]. The two conditions were balanced on gender, age, ethnic background, pain level recorded at baseline, and RDC axis I diagnoses." Comment: The study explicitly reports a computerized urn randomization procedure, which is a systematic random allocation method and is sufficient to judge the sequence generation as truly random.

Quote: "Those who agreed to participate were randomized to either the Standard Treatment group (STD; n=49) or to the Standard Treatment + Cognitive-Behavioral Treatment group (STD+CBT; n=52) using a computerized urn randomization procedure [28]. ... The Project Coordinator entered the urn data during the intake session and informed the participants of their treatment assignments." Comment: The report explicitly describes a computerized urn randomization procedure with the Project Coordinator entering data during intake and then informing participants of assignments. Although it does not mention opaque envelopes or central telephone/web allocation, the described process indicates that assignment was generated/managed at the time of enrollment rather than from an open schedule, and there is no indication that the recruiter could foresee the next assignment. Therefore this is judged low risk for allocation concealment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The first treatment appointment was then scheduled for one to two weeks later, coinciding with the delivery of the splint." "Patients in the STD+CBT condition received weekly CBT, STD patients received weekly 'progress checks'... The therapist took care to not deliver any kind of cognitive-behavioral treatment to these patients." Comment: Participants and therapists were not blinded to treatment allocation; the interventions were visibly different (CBT sessions versus progress checks, plus CBT content only in one arm). Because the main outcomes were self-reported pain, depression, and interference, knowledge of group assignment could plausibly influence outcomes and behavior, so the study is at high risk of performance bias.

Quote: "A trained M.A.-level research associate, who was not blinded to treatment condition, conducted the pretreatment and follow-up research assessments." Comment: The outcome assessor was explicitly unblinded. The main outcomes included patient-reported pain, depressive symptoms, and interference, all of which are subjective and could be influenced by the assessor’s knowledge of allocation, so detection bias is likely.

Quote: "At posttreatment 88% of patients provided data, and 73% provided data at 52 weeks. Losses to follow-up were equivalent across treatment conditions." Comment: Attrition was moderate but not excessive, and the paper explicitly states that losses were equivalent across groups. The authors also used a mixed model regression procedure with an intent-to-treat approach, which appropriately uses available data and reduces risk of attrition bias.
